Leveraging AI for Inventory Management and Accurate Forecast – An
Industrial Field Study
Morgan Edward Eldred, Jimmy Thatcher, Abdul Rehman, Ivan Gee, and Abhijith Suboyin, Digital Energy
Copyright 2023, Society of Petroleum Engineers DOI 10.2118/214457-MS
This paper was prepared for presentation at the SPE Symposium: Leveraging Artificial Intelligence to Shape the Future of the Energy Industry held in Al Khobar, Saudi
Arabia, 17 - 18 January 2023. The official proceedings were published online on 19 January, 2023.
This paper was selected for presentation by an SPE program committee following review of information contained in an abstract submitted by the author(s). Contents
of the paper have not been reviewed by the Society of Petroleum Engineers and are subject to correction by the author(s). The material does not necessarily reflect

Abstract
Accurately forecasting demand is one of the most undervalued and complex strategies that can significantly
impact organizations bottom line. This industrial field study was co-conducted with Sumitomo Corporation's
Tubular Division which primarily deals with high-grade Oil Country Tubular Goods (OCTG) globally.
The presented solution demonstrates how with the right data set (drilling sequence data, stock data and
consumption data), artificial intelligence can be used to build out a model that can quantify and predict
future demand accurately thereby reducing cost, working capital and emissions.
Multiple multi-layered machine learning models were built to compare and analyze a wide variety of data
inputs for bill of materials, operational/project schedules; This includes (a) ‘product movement data’ which
describes the changes in demand and supply of a product, (b) ‘product specification data’ which describes
the characteristics of a product, and (c) ‘activity specification data’ which describes the characteristics of an
activity. The models follow the base temporal map design with different weighting on model inputs. With a
temporal map, a sequence of monthly data values (called lags) is used to predict the next monthly value in
the sequence. The lags are rolled so that there are six months of data for the model to predict on. All models
also use boosted decision-tree-based ensemble machine learning algorithm.
It is critical to understand how product movement metrics (actual and safety stock levels, historical
forecasts, and consumption patterns), product specification data (lead time, product grade, well function,
well category, work center), and external factors (oil price, rig counts, national budget, production targets)
can be utilized together to better understand future product demand. Using historical data acquired from
drilling operations and supply chain over an eight-year period, multiple machine learning models were
trained to predict one year of demand across the most consumed products. Across five years of predictions
(2016 to 2019), the models were able to predict with 78% average accuracy for the top 10 products by
volume which represents 75% of inventory volume. Across the same time-period, they were able to predict
with 73% average accuracy on all 17 products which account for 80% percent of inventory volume. Further
iterative updates with additional data led to improvement in results and the model where the model predicted
with an improved accuracy of 83% on the top 17 products and an accuracy of 86% on the top 10 products.
Moreover, the data can also be used to generate dashboards featuring metrics on material uncertainty /
velocity and expected differences between the internally predicted forecasts and actual sales. The results

further indicate that, on average, and within a simulated environment (where shipping delays were not
considered for instance,) the AI model can maintain a lower inventory than the originally planned stock
levels at lowest cost and footprint. This would not only lead to less resource consumption, but also reduce
the embodied carbon and emissions within the overall process.
This novel study presents the success of a validated tailored AI model for inventory forecast with field
data and commercial implementation. Such a tool can be integrated into other value adding digital tools,
such as integrated schedule optimization, logistics optimization and management systems to make overall
operations more efficient and sustainable with lower costs, inventory, wastage, and reduced emissions.
Keywords: Artificial Intelligence, Optimization, Economics, Inventory Management, Emissions
Introduction
With the increasing energy demand, geopolitical constraints, and volatile market conditions, supply chain
operations in the hydrocarbon industry have become more complex over the past few years. As we head
towards an era of greater uncertainty associated with increasing risk, competitiveness, and scarcity of
resources; accurately forecasting demand is one of the most undervalued and complex strategies that can
significantly impact organizations bottom line.
The success of an exemplary inventory management solutions often relies on an organization's ability,
capability, and capacity to streamline and integrate the complete end to end process effectively. This may
include manufacturing, sourcing, processing, transportation, and end-delivery to the clients. With the advent
of intelligent solutions, organizations have realized the importance of take advantage of real-time data to
make informed decisions within supply chain that can significantly affect the company's bottom line. One
such implementation is through the application of artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning solutions
that can assist organizations to make complex decision in real-time through a data centric approach.
Over the years, there has been numerous promising applications through AI particularly for oil and gas
applications [1–10]. Even so, there are considerable gas and multiple domains where there is significant
potential to enhance current processes in place as illustrated in Figure 1. These include challenges related
to involving inventory management, purchasing, location planning, freight consolidation, and routing/
scheduling problems. This industrial field study was co-conducted with one of the dominant global
suppliers, Sumitomo Corporation's Tubular Division, which focuses on supplying a wide variety of tubular
products including high-grade Oil Country Tubular Goods (OCTG), OCTG accessories and line pipe to
companies in the oil and gas industries. In addition to their expertise in their proprietary supply chain
management system which provides comprehensive well management services, they aim to expand the
value chain beyond traditional services including demand forecasting, as it is one of the most undervalued
strategies for increasing profitability. Accurate forecasting benefits businesses in many ways that ultimately
increase the bottom line. Therefore, it is critical to understand how product movement metrics (actual and
safety stock levels, historical forecasts, and consumption patterns), product specification data (lead time,
product grade, well function, well category, work center), and external factors (oil price, rig counts, national
budget, production targets) can be used together to better understand future product demand. Such a tool
can be integrated into other value adding digital tools, such as integrated schedule optimization, fleet and
route optimization and connected Driller/worker. The industrial field study presented within the manuscript
demonstrates how with the right data set (drilling sequence data, stock data and consumption data), artificial
intelligence can be used to build out a model that can quantify and predict future demand accurately while
reducing the associated emissions. Hence, the major objectives of this paper are to:
a.
Demonstrate how AI can be used to better quantify and predict future demand accurately. This includes
using historical data effectively to improve accuracy of future orders in place along with reducing
lead times.

Results and Discussion
For the model development, an agile approach was taken. Each iteration cycle varied in length depending
on the learnings of each development cycle. The result was three cycles, where cycles one and two were
preliminary cycles to understand better how the model could be formed, how the model interprets the data,
and what our performance potential was. Each cycle followed a minimum of a 3–4-week development
cycle. When sufficient learnings or performance were achieved, an enhanced approach would improve
performance and accuracy, or closer align the model output with the business case and requirements. The
models were tested on the Top 17 products, which approximately represent 80% of the total product volume
consumed for the industrial application.
The tables each contain the following information: Data trained on, Year Predicted and Results.
a.
‘Data trained on’ refers to the data that the model has available to learn from during that cycle. For
instance, if data trained on is 2011 – 2014, that means the model had the data (drilling sequence data,
stock data and consumption data) from 2011 through 2014 to learn from.
b.
‘Year predicted’ refers to the year that the model was tested on before seeing the actual results. For
instance, if year predicted is 2015, then the model was used to predict 2015 results before those results
were released. This can ensure that the model did not learn anything about the 2015 results before
the 2015 predictions were made.
c.
Results are given in terms of Mean Absolute Percent Error or MAPE, a common way of reporting
results with aggregated percent errors. MAPE forces the use of an absolute value for the percent error
when averaging across multiple percent errors. This ensures that positive and negative errors do not
cancel each other out and gives a much more accurate average percent error value. For instance, if a
MAPE is reported as 45% across the top 17 products, this means that the percent error was calculated
for each product, each percent error was made positive (a -15% error become a +15% error so that
the -15 and +15 percent errors did not cancel each other out in the calculation) and finally the mean
of these errors was calculated to arrive at the final answer. The formula for MAPE is given below:
M = mean absolute percentage error
n = number of times the summation iteration happens
At = actual Value
Ft = forecast Value
Accuracy is then calculated as 1-MAPE.
The final models were evaluated for accuracy and performance in predicting 2016, 2017, 2018, and 2019
total consumption and in predicting total consumption grouped into slow, medium, and fast movers. The
two tables below show the results.

• Across five years of predictions (2016 to 2019), the models were able to predict with 78% average
accuracy for the top 10 products by volume which represents 75% of inventory volume. Across the
same time-period, they were able to predict with 73% average accuracy on all 17 products which
account for 80% percent of inventory volume.
• Further iterative updates with additional data led to improvement in results and the model where
the model predicted with an improved accuracy of 83% on the top 17 products and an accuracy
of 86% on the top 10 products.
• AI model provides a way to identify where the traditional forecast might be off or misleading and
provides the necessary information to challenge it along with specifying how to correct it.
• Furthermore, the AI model within a simulated environment (shipping delays where not considered
for instance,) shows potential to maintain a lower inventory than the current stock levels.
The results generated from this investigation shows considerable promise for accurate forecasting
demand with the right data sets and techniques. Further iterations of the model aim to expand the learnings
to optimizations within logistics, drilling sequence, end to end emission tracking within supply chain and
enhancing HSE through ‘Smart Alerts’ to make informed business decisions while making operations more
sustainable.
